goblet-squat.name: Goblet squat
cues: Hold the dumbbell tight against your chest
Sit down between your heels, not back
Elbows track inside your knees at the bottom

teachingConcept.title: Bracing
teachingConcept.body: Before each rep, take a breath into your belly and gently tense your midsection — like preparing for a light poke. A braced trunk keeps your spine stable so your legs can do the work.

split-squat.name: Split squat
cues: Long stance, back heel free
Drop the back knee straight down
Front foot carries most of the weight

tempo-bodyweight-squat.name: Tempo bodyweight squat
cues: Three seconds down
Pause briefly at the bottom
Stand tall between reps

bench-press.name: Barbell bench press
cues: Pull your shoulder blades down and together
Slight arch, feet planted
Lower with control to mid-chest

teachingConcept.title: Scapular position
teachingConcept.body: Set your shoulder blades down and back before unracking, and keep them there. A stable scapula turns the bench into a launch pad and protects your shoulders.

deficit-push-up.name: Deficit push-up
cues: Hands elevated on books or handles
Chest below hand level at the bottom
Body is one rigid line

db-floor-press.name: Dumbbell floor press
cues: Elbows touch the floor softly each rep
Press slightly inward
Wrists stacked over elbows

dumbbell-bench-press.name: Dumbbell bench press
cues: Keep the dumbbells roughly shoulder-width apart, never touching
Elbows track at about 45° to your torso, not flared to 90°
Wrists stay stacked over the elbows all the way down

teachingConcept.title: Independent control
teachingConcept.body: Two dumbbells can drift apart if you're not paying attention — press them on the same path every rep, not just to the same height. That independent stability is exactly what a dumbbell bench press trains that a barbell can't.

incline-dumbbell-press.name: Incline dumbbell press
cues: Bench set to roughly 30° — press stays along the line of the torso, not straight up
Elbows track at about 45° to your torso, tucked under the wrists at the bottom
Dumbbells finish over your upper chest, not your face

teachingConcept.title: Torso line, not vertical
teachingConcept.body: On an incline the press path isn't straight up — it follows the angle of the bench, finishing over your upper chest and collarbones. Press toward your face and you lose the whole point of the incline: shifting emphasis to the upper chest.

single-arm-db-row.name: Single-arm dumbbell row
cues: Flat back, hips square
Pull to your hip, not your armpit
Let the shoulder blade move — reach, then row

bent-over-row.name: Barbell bent-over row
cues: Hinge to ~45°
Pull to your lower ribs
Pause at the top

band-row.name: Band row
cues: Anchor at chest height
Squeeze the shoulder blades
Slow return

romanian-deadlift.name: Romanian deadlift
cues: Push your hips back, soft knees
Bar stays close to your legs
Stop when your hamstrings say stop

teachingConcept.title: The hip hinge
teachingConcept.body: A hinge moves your hips backward while your spine stays long — it is a bow, not a squat. Mastering it loads your hamstrings and glutes instead of your lower back, and it carries into almost every lift.

single-leg-rdl.name: Single-leg Romanian deadlift
cues: Hips stay square to the floor
Reach the free leg back
Slow and balanced beats heavy

dumbbell-rdl.name: Dumbbell Romanian deadlift
cues: Knee bend is set once at the top and doesn't change — this is a hinge, not a squat
Dumbbells stay in contact with your legs all the way down
Hips travel back, not down — stop where your hamstrings tell you to

teachingConcept.title: Same hinge, closer to the body
teachingConcept.body: Dumbbells sit closer to your legs than a barbell would, so let them slide down your thighs and shins the whole way — don't let them drift forward. Stop when your hamstrings say stop, not at a fixed height.

bulgarian-split-squat.name: Bulgarian split squat
cues: Rear foot on the bench, laces down
Torso tall, slight forward lean is fine
Drive through the whole front foot

overhead-press.name: Barbell overhead press
cues: Squeeze glutes, ribs down
Bar travels in a straight line — move your head, not the bar
Finish with biceps by your ears

teachingConcept.title: Ribs down
teachingConcept.body: Overarching your lower back turns an overhead press into a lower-back exercise. Tuck your ribs toward your hips and squeeze your glutes — the press should come from shoulders, not spine.

single-arm-db-press.name: Single-arm dumbbell press
cues: Full-body tension
Press slightly back, not forward
Resist side-bending

dumbbell-shoulder-press.name: Dumbbell shoulder press
cues: Ribs stay down — no leaning back as the weights pass overhead
Wrists stay stacked over the elbows the whole way up
Both dumbbells finish at the same height, every rep

teachingConcept.title: Ribs down, not back
teachingConcept.body: It's tempting to lean back and turn this into an incline press once the weight gets heavy. Keep your ribs pulled down toward your hips throughout — that's what keeps the load on your shoulders instead of your lower back.

hip-thrust.name: Hip thrust
cues: Shoulder blades on the bench edge
Chin tucked, ribs down at the top
Squeeze for a full second at lockout

single-leg-hip-thrust.name: Single-leg hip thrust
cues: Hips stay level
Drive through the heel
Slow on the way down

band-pull-apart.name: Band pull-apart
cues: Arms long, band to chest
Squeeze between the shoulder blades
Control the return

rear-delt-fly.name: Rear delt fly
cues: Hip hinge angle stays fixed — don't rise to help lift the weight
Elbow bend stays soft and constant — this is a fly, not a row
Stop level with your torso at the top, don't swing past it

teachingConcept.title: A fly, not a row
teachingConcept.body: The moment your elbows start bending to pull the weight in, the movement stops training your rear delts and starts recruiting your upper back instead. Keep that elbow angle locked and let the sweep come from the shoulder.

band-lateral-raise.name: Band lateral raise
cues: Lead with the elbows
Stop at shoulder height
No swinging

band-curl.name: Band curl
cues: Elbows pinned to your sides
Squeeze at the top
Three seconds down

dead-bug.name: Dead bug
cues: Lower back gently pressed into the floor
Opposite arm and leg reach away slowly
Exhale as you extend

side-plank.name: Side plank
cues: Elbow under shoulder
Hips tall — one straight line
Breathe steadily

barbell-squat.name: Barbell back squat
cues: Bar sits on your upper traps, not your neck
Break at the hips and knees together, sit between your heels
Drive the floor away evenly through both feet to stand

teachingConcept.title: Bar path
teachingConcept.body: The bar should travel in a straight vertical line over the middle of your foot. If your hips shoot up first, you turn the squat into a hip hinge — sit down, not back, and let your hips and shoulders rise together.

cable-row.name: Seated cable row
cues: Sit tall, chest up, slight forward lean at the start
Pull to your lower ribs, elbows close to your body
Let your shoulder blades protract fully before the next rep

teachingConcept.title: Lead with the elbow
teachingConcept.body: Initiate every rep by driving your elbow back, not by curling the handle in with your arm. Your back does the pulling; your bicep just holds on.

lat-pulldown.name: Lat pulldown
cues: Grip just outside shoulder width
Pull the bar to your upper chest, elbows driving down and back
Control the return — don't let the weight stack pull your arms up

teachingConcept.title: Down and back, not just down
teachingConcept.body: Think about pulling your elbows toward your back pockets, not just pulling the bar down. That cue shifts the work into your lats instead of your arms.

barbell-hip-thrust.name: Barbell hip thrust
cues: Upper back braced on the bench edge, bar padded across your hips
Chin tucked, ribs down as you drive up
Full lockout — squeeze glutes hard for a second at the top

teachingConcept.title: Bar placement
teachingConcept.body: The bar should sit in the crease of your hips, not on your lower belly — a thick pad or folded mat protects that spot. If it feels like it's crushing you, it's in the wrong place, not too heavy.

dumbbell-lateral-raise.name: Dumbbell lateral raise
cues: Slight bend in the elbows, held throughout
Raise to shoulder height, leading with the elbows
Lower slowly — the negative is where this exercise earns its keep

teachingConcept.title: Light weight, real range
teachingConcept.body: This is not a heavy-load exercise — momentum from swinging the weight up removes tension from the shoulder, which is the entire point of the movement. If you need to lean or swing to lift it, the weight is too heavy.

dumbbell-curl.name: Dumbbell curl
cues: Elbows pinned to your sides through the whole rep
Squeeze at the top without swinging the weight up
Lower under control through the full range

teachingConcept.title: Elbow position is everything
teachingConcept.body: If your elbows drift forward as the weight gets heavy, your front delts take over from your biceps. Keep them stacked under your shoulders for the whole set, even if that means picking a lighter dumbbell.

romanian-deadlift.note: Slow eccentric — the bar takes 3 seconds down

bent-over-row.note: Pause at the top of each rep

single-leg-hip-thrust.note: Keep the hips level — the free leg stays extended
